abstract: Pixar Animation Studios, whose joint venture with Walt Disney Co is set to expire in 2005, has yet to begin serious talks to determine its next distribution partner; chief executive Steven P Jobs cites outlook for shifts in top executives at studios; planned retirement of Michael D Eisner as Disney chief executive seen key factor; Jobs has always favored deal with Disney, but blamed Eisner for souring two companies' lucrative ties; photo (M)

abstract: Outlook for famous companies like Merrill Lynch and Morgan Stanley discussed in light of encroachments by universal banks such as Citigroup and J P Morgan, with their swollen balance sheets and ripe ambitions; investment bankers say two old-line firms are in similar quandaries but that Merrill faces most pressure to merge because it has become so much smaller; cite upcoming public offering of DreamWorks SKG's animation unit as example of how big banks use financial brawn and deal-making skill to muscle in on territory long owned by investment banks; photos; graphs showing stock declines this year for major Wall Street firms (M)

abstract: Architect Daniel Libeskind, hailed last year as master planner of World Trade Center site, now plays far less visible role in redevelopment of downtown Manhattan; his influence, control and stature have steadily eroded as signature elements of his plan have been altered, reduced or eliminated; planning is now dominated by Port Authority of New York and New Jersey, which owns site, and by Skidmore, Owings & Merrill, architectural firm hired by Larry A Silverstein, site's leaseholder; Libeskind reportedly no longer attends meetings at which plans are discussed; photos (L)
